message.content:
You are confirmer #2, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (for a code change the named test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if reverted; for a doc/decision item, judge whether the documented claim is now accurate/honest). Repo /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix H-023 in src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
What: redact_creds docstring corrected: precise only when conn string appears verbatim; regex backstops otherwise.
Test(s): (doc; no test)
Read the actual changed file(s); read the test; check no regression. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN. Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

commit f4c27988c738afa0a04c67abe28f529895a64bb7
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Mon Jun 15 22:49:25 2026 -0400

    feat(persistence): checkpointer + vector store + local data versioning (#49)
    
    * feat(persistence): port checkpointer + vector store + local data versioning
    
    Third infra port (ADR-0003), generalized from hc-grc; dataset-agnostic
    (ASSAY_* env), backends optional (persistence extra) and lazily imported.
    
    - checkpoint.py: hardened connection handling — loopback-enforced connection
      string (env override guarded at point of use), two-layer credential
      redaction (exact conn_str replacement + URI-userinfo regex backstop, robust
      to '/'/'#'/space passwords and IPv6), and a lazy LangGraph checkpointer
      factory (memory|postgres) that redacts creds from any setup error.
    - vectorstore.py: loopback-enforced Qdrant factory (guard fires before import).
    - versioning.py: LocalDataVersioner — dependency-free SHA-256 content-addressed
      store with atomic publish (deterministic, fully offline; an adapter may swap
      in DVC via the same Protocol).
    
    Tests 137 -> 153; ruff + mypy --strict clean (30 files).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    * fix(persistence): restore the hardened checkpointer + document the design (ADR-0006/0007)
    
    Reasoned rework after a self-caught quality regression: the first cut silently
    dropped hc-grc's hardened checkpointer machinery and replaced it with a naive
    from_conn_string().setup() — no pooling, DDL re-run every call, no cross-process
    serialization. For a blueprint that 100+ studies inherit and that runs
    multi-process interrupt/resume across approval gates, each dropped behavior is
    load-bearing, so they are ported faithfully (generalized, ASSAY_* env, no
    config-file coupling) with the rationale kept in the code:
    
    - self-healing ConnectionPool (a raw connection loses durability when Postgres
      blips mid-gate); liveness check on checkout.
    - session advisory lock serializing first-time schema setup across processes,
      run on the SAME connection as the DDL (a pooled setup() would lock a different
      connection and be ineffective).
    - once-per-process-per-conn bootstrap guard with an in-process lock making the
      check-and-set atomic; single shared atexit pool cleanup (avoids
      PythonFinalizationError and per-call handler accumulation).
    - credential redaction hardened: _sanitize_conn_str now guards ValueError from
      urlsplit().port on malformed userinfo ('/'/'#'/space passwords, IPv6); error
      raised `from None` so the credential-bearing text can't reappear via __cause__.
    
    Removed the redundant save/load Checkpointer Protocol — it duplicated and
    diverged from LangGraph's real BaseCheckpointSaver; two checkpointer contracts
    would mislead cloners. The factory returns the genuine LangGraph saver.
    
    Data versioning: fixed an atomic-publish race (concurrent putters of the same
    digest shared one .tmp path) — now a per-writer-unique tempfile + os.replace,
    with leftover cleanup; documented edge cases.
    
    New ADRs (decisions every clone inherits):
    - ADR-0006: heavy backends optional via lazy imports + extras; fail-loud at
      point of use; pure security/correctness logic tested offline.
    - ADR-0007: engine-native content-addressed data versioning; external VCS
      optional via the DataVersioner Protocol in an adapter.
    
    Tests 153 -> 155 (+ memory-path fail-loud, no-leftover-temp); ruff + mypy
    --strict clean (30 files).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    * fix(persistence): resolve 3 audit findings (DSN loopback bypass, __context__ leak, coverage)
    
    - P1 (high): require_local_uri now recognizes the libpq keyword/value DSN form
      (host=...) and enforces loopback on its host token — a remote DSN no longer
      bypasses data sovereignty. Restructured to check urlparse host first (covers
      scheme-relative //host too), then DSN, then bare path.
    - P2 (medium): the redacted connection error is now raised OUTSIDE the except
      handler, so the credential-bearing original is retained as neither __cause__
      nor __context__; added a DSN password=[REDACTED] redaction layer; corrected
      the docstring.
    - P3 (medium): added tests (fake backends injected) for once-per-conn setup,
      advisory-lock-on-same-connection, atexit single-registration, the DSN
      loopback rejection, DSN-password redaction, and the __context__ no-leak.
    
    Tests 155 -> 161; ruff + mypy --strict clean (30 files).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    * fix(persistence): close residual DSN loopback bypass via whitespace/quoting (D1)
    
    Independent fix-review of P1 found the first fix incomplete: libpq permits
    whitespace around '=' (host = db.evil.com), tabs, and quoted values, which a
    split()/partition tokenizer misses — the remote host slipped through. Replace
    with a whitespace/quote-tolerant regex tokenizer that loopback-checks
    host/hostaddr captures. Parametrized regression tests cover all spellings
    (space before/after/around '=', tabs, single-quoted, hostaddr, HOST=).
    
    Tests 161 -> 170; ruff + mypy --strict clean (30 files).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    * fix(persistence): close URI query host/hostaddr loopback bypass (D2, parser differential)
    
    A focused break-test (validated against real libpq PQconninfoParse) found that
    require_local_uri trusted only urlparse's authority host, but a postgresql:// URI
    query host=/hostaddr= overrides it in libpq — so postgresql://localhost/x?hostaddr=8.8.8.8
    passed the guard while libpq would dial 8.8.8.8. Critical off-box exfiltration path.
    
    Fix: in the URI branch, parse_qs the query and reject any non-loopback host or
    hostaddr (checked independently — a loopback host does not make a remote hostaddr
    safe). Parametrized regression tests cover query-host override, hostaddr-only,
    host+hostaddr, IPv6 authority, and userinfo forms.
    
    Tests 170 -> 177; ruff + mypy --strict clean (30 files).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    * fix(persistence): close multi-host authority bypass + UNC/fail-closed hardening (D3/D4)
    
    Second break-test (validated against libpq PQconninfoParse) found a 4th bypass
    of the same data-sovereignty guard:
    
    - D3 (high): libpq multi-host URI authority (host1,host2) — urlparse returns only
      the first host, so postgresql://localhost:5432,evil.com:5432/db passed while
      libpq dials evil.com. require_local_uri now parses the raw authority and
      loopback-checks EVERY host (userinfo stripped, comma-split, [ipv6]:port aware).
    - D4 (low): reject Windows UNC paths (\\server\share = remote SMB) in the
      bare-path branch; wrap urlparse so a malformed authority fails closed with
      NonLocalEndpointError instead of a bare ValueError.
    
    The URI branch now validates against what the real client connects to (every
    authority host + query host/hostaddr) rather than urlparse's single host.
    
    Tests 177 -> 184; ruff + mypy --strict clean (30 files).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    * fix(persistence): align userinfo parsing with libpq first-@ semantics (D5)
    
    Third break-test (validated via libpq PQhost) found a 5th differential: the guard
    stripped userinfo at the LAST @ (urlparse semantics) but libpq uses the FIRST @,
    so postgresql://[REDACTED]@evil.com:5432@localhost/db resolved to evil.com in libpq
    while the guard saw localhost. _authority_hosts now splits at the first @ and the
    authority loop fails closed on any residual @ (ambiguous). Tests cover the
    differential shapes; ordinary userinfo@host still accepted.
    
    Tests 184 -> 189; ruff + mypy --strict clean (30 files).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    * fix(persistence): separator-agnostic UNC rejection + no backslash in authority (D6)
    
    Final convergence break-test found a Windows-only edge: any two leading
    separators (mixed \ and /) form a UNC prefix to a remote SMB share, but the
    guard only caught literal \\. Now reject any path whose first two non-space
    chars are separators. Also reject backslashes in a URI authority (a WHATWG HTTP
    client treats \ as / and could resolve a different host than urlparse) — fail
    closed, defense-in-depth. The Postgres/libpq parser-differential surface, where
    the prior five bypasses lived, was confirmed convergent.
    
    Tests 189 -> 193; ruff + mypy --strict clean (30 files).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    * chore(persistence): address review nits — declare langgraph core dep; ADR-0007 retrieval caveat
    
    Final review (APPROVE_WITH_NITS): (1) declare langgraph core explicitly in the
    persistence extra rather than relying on it transitively via the postgres
    checkpoint package; (2) document in ADR-0007 that retrieval-by-id (path_for) is
    store-specific and deliberately not part of the DataVersioner Protocol.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>
    
    ---------
    
    Co-authored-by: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
    Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py b/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
index ed6e52b..95ab490 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
@@ -1,18 +1,229 @@
-"""Durable checkpointer seam (contract + stub).
+"""Durable run-state checkpointer — local, data-sovereign (ADR-0003).
 
-Persists run state at phase boundaries so a study resumes deterministically. Backed by a
-local database when wired (lifted from the prior platform's durable checkpointer).
+The orchestration graph persists its full state (current phase, gate decisions, locked
+hypotheses, verdicts) through a checkpointer so a run can interrupt at a human-approval gate
+and resume later. That state holds the entire analysis, so the store is **on-box only**
+(ADR-0003); a SaaS checkpointer is disqualified.
+
+This module is the LangGraph checkpointer factory, ported faithfully from the prior
+platform's hardened implementation and generalized (dataset-agnostic ``ASSAY_*`` env, no
+config-file coupling — see ADR-0006). The hardened behaviors are deliberately preserved
+because each prevents a real production failure:
+
+- a **self-healing ConnectionPool** (not a raw connection): when Postgres goes away — node
+  sleep, restart, idle timeout, a network blip — a cached raw connection is permanently
+  broken and every later checkpoint op raises, i.e. durability is lost exactly during the
+  interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to support. The pool runs a liveness check on
+  checkout and replaces dead connections;
+- a **session advisory lock** serializing first-time schema setup across *processes*, run on

content.content:
commit 011942ba0d024960245c8ab919fdf86388cff686
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 01:31:14 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p5): batch B — reliability, CI, observability, docs (13 findings)
    
    Pass-5 confirmed findings (2-agent verified after self-refutation; revert-discriminated):
    
    Reliability / error contracts:
    - H-004 (med) malformed reasoning params (temperature/seed) raise the typed
      PermanentReasoningError instead of a raw ValueError/TypeError leaking past run()/run_json.
    - H-021 (low) QdrantVectorStore.query rejects non-positive k and skips returned points
      lacking our 'ref' payload (was an opaque KeyError).
    - H-022 (low) vectorstore int env vars parse via _int_env, naming the bad var instead of an
      opaque import-time "invalid literal for int()".
    - H-013 (low) the provenance-trail artifact tempfile is unlinked after the tracker copies it
      (was delete=False, leaked per tracked run).
    
    CI / supply-chain:
    - H-007 (med) license gate surfaces UNKNOWN/empty-license packages as a ::warning:: (hidden-
      copyleft vector) instead of passing them silently; still fails only on explicit deny matches.
    - H-008 (med) workflow pins GITHUB_TOKEN to least-privilege `permissions: contents: read`.
    - H-009 (med) the integration job verifies the Postgres/Qdrant service containers are
      reachable (pg_isready + /readyz) so the live tests can't silently SKIP and pass vacuously.
    
    Observability / governance:
    - H-011 (med) documented the deliberate recorder-optionality asymmetry between the graph gate
      node and run_study (the runner owns + always records to the trail; the node bridges).
    - H-017 (low) documented that serialized provenance records don't embed keying — the verifier
      supplies the secret out-of-band (key management; verify_records fails closed).
    
    Documentation honesty (assertion-vs-implementation):
    - H-010 (med) stable_seed: "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" corrected to the birthday
      bound of the truncated space (not a uniqueness guarantee).
    - H-012 (low) gatenode "atomically"/"terminal idempotency" claims scoped honestly.
    - H-023 (low) redact_creds "always scrubbed precisely" corrected to "precise when the conn
      string appears verbatim" (reformatted strings fall to the regex backstops).
    - H-018 (med) transcript scrubber redacts the username in the project-dir slug at any length.
    - H-024 (low) the in-process unfreeze test is marked illustrative; the seed-pinned subprocess
      test is the discriminating guard.
=== DIFF for checkpoint.py in 011942b ===

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py b/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
index 41b855e..d779b73 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
@@ -137,7 +137,15 @@ def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
     3. a libpq DSN ``password=[REDACTED] strip for the keyword/value form.
 
     Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
-    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
+    not pass as ``conn_str``.
+
+    Precision caveat (pass 5, #H-023): layer 1 is an EXACT-substring replacement, so it scrubs the
+    primary connection string precisely only when that string appears verbatim in ``text``. If a
+    backend reformats it (re-quoting, reordering DSN keywords, percent-encoding the password) the
+    verbatim match misses and only the layers-2–3 regex backstops apply — which still strip
+    ``://[REDACTED]@`` userinfo and ``password=[REDACTED] tokens, but are not guaranteed exhaustive for
+    every backend message shape. It is "precise when the conn string appears verbatim", not
+    "always precise".
     """
     if conn_str:
         text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))

128	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
129	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
130	
131	    Layers, in order of precision:
132	
133	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
134	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
135	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://[REDACTED]@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
136	       *other* URI in the text;
137	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=[REDACTED] strip for the keyword/value form.
138	
139	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
140	    not pass as ``conn_str``.
141	
142	    Precision caveat (pass 5, #H-023): layer 1 is an EXACT-substring replacement, so it scrubs the
143	    primary connection string precisely only when that string appears verbatim in ``text``. If a
144	    backend reformats it (re-quoting, reordering DSN keywords, percent-encoding the password) the
145	    verbatim match misses and only the layers-2–3 regex backstops apply — which still strip
146	    ``://[REDACTED]@`` userinfo and ``password=[REDACTED] tokens, but are not guaranteed exhaustive for
147	    every backend message shape. It is "precise when the conn string appears verbatim", not
148	    "always precise".
149	    """
150	    if conn_str:
151	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
152	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
153	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
154	
155	
156	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
157	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
158	
159	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
160	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
161	    """
162	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
163	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
164	
165	
166	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
167	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
168	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
169	    that can raise)."""
170	    try:
171	        pool.close()
172	    except Exception:
173	        pass
174	
175	
176	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
177	    # Snapshot under the meta-lock before iterating (#F-020): _OPEN_POOLS is appended under
178	    # _init_lock elsewhere, and on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build a concurrent list.append
179	    # reallocation during iteration is undefined behavior. _safe_close never takes _init_lock, so
180	    # closing outside the lock cannot deadlock.
181	    with _init_lock:
182	        pools = list(_OPEN_POOLS)
183	    for p in pools:
184	        _safe_close(p)
185	
186	
187	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
188	    global _atexit_registered
189	    with _init_lock:
190	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
191	        if not _atexit_registered:
192	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
193	            _atexit_registered = True
194	
195	
196	def _conn_init_lock(conn_str: str) -> threading.Lock:
197	    """Return the per-conn_str bootstrap lock, creating it under the brief meta-lock (#129)."""
198	    with _init_lock:
199	        lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS.get(conn_str)
200	        if lock is None:
201	            lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = threading.Lock()
202	        return lock
203	
204	
205	def _cached_pool(conn_str: str) -> Any:
206	    with _init_lock:
207	        return _POOLS_BY_CONN.get(conn_str)
208	
209

===

# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
_atexit_registered = False

# Strips ``://[REDACTED]@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[REDACTED]@\s]+@")
# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=[REDACTED] / ``pgpassword=...``),
# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")


def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.

    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
    """
    try:
        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
        host = u.hostname or ""
        port = u.port
        scheme = u.scheme
        path = u.path
    except ValueError:
        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
    if not host:
        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
        host = f"[{host}]"
    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"

content.text:
Now I'll verify each docstring claim against the implementation.

The docstring claims:
1. Layer 1 is exact-substring replacement (`text.replace(conn_str, ...)`) — confirmed at line 151. Precise only when conn_str appears verbatim. Correct: `str.replace` is literal substring matching, so a reformatted conn string won't match.
2. Layers 2-3 strip `://[REDACTED]@` userinfo (`_CREDS_RE` at line 99) and `password=[REDACTED] tokens (`_DSN_PW_RE` at line 102) — confirmed at lines 152-153.
3. "not guaranteed exhaustive for every backend message shape" — accurate. The regexes only catch URI-userinfo form and `password=[REDACTED] keywords. A password embedded in another shape (e.g., a positional value, or a percent-encoded password where verbatim match fails but `://[REDACTED]@` still matches userinfo... actually `_CREDS_RE` would still catch percent-encoded userinfo) is not guaranteed covered.

Let me sanity-check the percent-encoding example in the docstring: percent-encoding the password — `_CREDS_RE` (`://[REDACTED]@\s]+@`) would still match `://[REDACTED]@host` since `%` is not `@`/whitespace. So even on a reformat, userinfo gets stripped. The docstring acknowledges backstops "still strip `://[REDACTED]@` userinfo" — consistent. The caveat is honest about not being exhaustive.

The previous claim ("the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely") was indeed an overstatement, since `str.replace` fails on any reformat. The new docstring corrects this accurately. This is a doc-only change; no test required, no behavior change, no regression.

Verifying the original (reverted) claim was indeed inaccurate: "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely" — false, because layer 1 (line 151) is `str.replace(conn_str, ...)`, a literal substring match that misses any reformatted rendering of the conn string. The new wording correctly scopes precision to verbatim appearance and accurately describes the regex backstops (`_CREDS_RE` for `://[REDACTED]@`, `_DSN_PW_RE` for `password=[REDACTED]).

The new docstring correctly scopes precision to "when the conn string appears verbatim" and accurately describes the regex backstops: _CREDS_RE = ://[REDACTED]@\s]+@ (line 99) strips URI userinfo, _DSN_PW_RE (line 102) strips password=[REDACTED] tokens (lines 152-153) — matching the docstring's stated layers. The honest caveat that backstops "are not guaranteed exhaustive for every backend message shape" is correct: a credential rendered in a shape outside URI-userinfo / password= [REDACTED] form is not covered.
